item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: SCP Foundation Wiki
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: IronGolem-new.jpg
Author: matsuri_ht
Release year: 2018

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So cute!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2300-99 was accidentally created in SCP-914 test 914-0998.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite sure I like the crosslink. I see the rationale for it but I think it doesn't really fit cleanly, so to speak.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Dwaaaa. Aren't these guys the cutest. And really useful from an analytical chemistry standpoint. I could see the SCPF using them to take apart samples with unknown elements to isolate said unknown elements, such as Telekill Alloy. I could also see the SCPF wanting to keep these guys as far away as possible from SCP-2046, due to the later's nasty habit of creating new anomalous elements. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2300 instances are currently individually contained within specially-designed containment units within Site ██. Containment units must not match the composition of the SCP-2300 instance they contain and must be resistant to damage caused by the contained instance's three states of matter. Radioactive instances of SCP-2300 are to be placed in a separate containment area with appropriate shielding.

To this:

SCP-2300 instances are currently individually contained within specially-designed containment units within Site ██. Containment units must not match the composition of the SCP-2300 instance they contain and must be resistant to damage caused by the contained instance's three states of matter. Radioactive instances of SCP-2300 are to be placed in a separate containment area with appropriate shielding. At no point should any instance of SCP-2300 and SCP-2046 be kept within the same facility.

Adding the following paragraph to the end:

Proposals to use instances of SCP-2300 as means of isolating anomalous elements from other materials, including SCP-148, are currently pending review.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this SCP appeals to me in the same way something like watching fish in a tank would. Watching a little civilization go about their daily life is entertaining if anything, it's a good contrast to the unending bloodlust that other SCPs commonly promote. It doesn't need to ask the question "how does it kill people? why does it kill people?" and I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why this is so well liked. Yes, there are some subtle chemistry puns, and there's some tacked-on metafiction, but the entire thing is, well, static. There's no story, or change, or new information revealed. While the meme is overused, I admit, this is just a Thing That Does A Thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is metafiction about SCP writers.
Golems(writers) make some gallery(SCP Series), and they display their artwork without feedback. Works that are universally viewed as poor(usually made by less experienced newbies) are often removed from gallery. How delicate is this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm. I was decidedly neutral vote for most of it. The stuff about the noble gasses being the nobles and dense elements being dumb was cute, but I wasn't exactly enthralled. Then I got to the last paragraph about their anart galleries and noticed the meta commentary, and I downvoted.

I think noob bashing in general is a little meanspirited. Constant references around the site and it's culture make new member's opinions out to be lesser in some way (not always, but very often). To see that sort of thing codified in an actual SCP just seems in poor taste to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They show up quite a bit in Learning the alphabet

Hub is at http://www.scp-wiki.net/learning-the-alphabet-hub

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love these little dudes and want to hear more ♥

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely well written, but as much as I love chemistry, the concept is a little too obvious and on the nose. I knew exactly where it was going after the title and first sentence, and it went exactly nowhere else except there.

I will refrain from downvoting because I have a soft spot for anything chemistry related, but this feels too simple and gimmicky for an upvote either.

Edit for definitive autocorrect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've only been out of high school for a year, and I'm already getting nostalgic. Damn you, TwistedGears, take all my +1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. I'm pretty sure that most of the time it should be referring to "instances of SCP-2300", not simply "SCP-2300", but other than that, this is quite good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To cute. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would this happen to be inspired by Order of the Stick?

If not, still a clever idea either way; if so, well done expanding the concept from a simple running gag.
Hey, congrats on a new century, TwistedGears! Way to go!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first 98 elements are naturally occurring, any higher and they decay too quickly.

Like we talked about in chat, hydrogen and helium do have solid states. They're just hard to reach and likely act a little… strangely compared to other solids. Plus, even if they didn't, the little dudes are anomalies so they'd arguably get a handwave on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why only 98 element men?

And another thought - I'm not sure some of the elements /have/ solid forms.

Hydrogen and helium being the two I don't remember having solid forms even at excessive pressure and near-absolute zero temperatures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agree with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was liking this until I hit this:

However, there appears to be no preliminary process to what can be in any given gallery; meaning less experienced SCP-2300 are capable of showcasing material deemed unsatisfactory to a majority of their more experienced peers. While works that are universally viewed as poor are often removed from galleries within a small timeframe, some are left on display due to the approval of the high number of less experienced SCP-2300.

Too cute by half. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely got that vibe.

Seconding this suspicion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome. I love the idea of a society of mini-skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
contained separately

… aww … :(

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That meta last paragraph doe.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is maybe the best part of the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it clever that you made the noble gases the nobility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspected that myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was ambivalent about a metal men (well, element men) SCP, but a sentient and animate periodic table is too funny to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not just metal. Elements from all over the periodic table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fairly simple one, but its fun and well-executed.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really like to see a more satisfactory ending for this, but it's still rather good, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, the metal men, huh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just me, or is the bit about the art gallery at the end (and some "less worthy" works staying on due to the approbation of newer members of SCP-2300) a reference to the wiki itself? :-)

+1 either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked these little fellas in the draft form, and I like it here! Good job! +1

I loved the chemistry allusions here as well! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it in the chat, liked it in here.
+1

Edit: Find the funny meta references!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've significantly improved the compositional style since the first time I saw this posted in chat. It's a lot less clunky.

Overall, the idea is interesting. I'm glad you removed the interview log - it was a bit too on the nose, even though I thought it added to the creep factor.

Good writing, a few subtle chemistry jokes and an adorable (yet still kinda creepy) SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I like it a lot. I like the thing you did with the noble gases, the whole "dense elements are dense," and all the other stuff. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very entertaining. You know, I had never thought of what the social structure of elements would be like. You've thought well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is certainly an interesting one. I like the idea of a GoI scip going beyond mere association with its group and actually forming a society, of sorts.
